text:
Published

Aug 03, 2024 at 06:08 PM EDT

Weekend Night Editor

In a long-awaited move, the Vatican has finally weighed in on the ongoing debate surrounding acontroversial segmentfrom the 2024 ParisOlympicsopening ceremony, issuing a rare weekend press release expressing disappointment over what many perceived as a parody of Leonardo da Vinci's iconic "The Last Supper" painting.

The statement, released Saturday evening in French reported byReuters, marked an unusual step for the Holy See, which typically refrains from commenting on cultural events outside of official channels.

"The Holy See was saddened by certain scenes at the opening ceremony of the Paris Olympic Games and cannot but join the voices raised in recent days to deplore the offence done to many Christians and believers of other religions," the Vatican said.

The controversy stems from a segment in the July 26 ceremony that featured a tableau reminiscent of the biblical scene depicting Jesus Christ's last meal with his apostles. However, the Olympic interpretation included drag queens, a transgender model, and a nude performer representing Dionysus, the Greek god of wine and revelry.

Newsweekcontacted the Holy See's Press Office via email on Saturday for further comment.

The Vatican's response comes more than a week after the event, raising questions about the timing of the statement.

While the Holy See did not provide a specific reason for the delay, some speculate it may be related to a recent phone call betweenPope Francisand Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan on August 1. The Vatican confirmed the call took place but declined to comment on its contents.

The statement emphasized the importance of respecting religious beliefs in global events like the Olympics.

"In a prestigious event where the whole world comes together around common values, there should not be allusions ridiculing the religious convictions of many people," the Vatican said. It also addressed the balance between freedom of expression and respect for others, stating, "Freedom of expression, which is obviously not called into question, finds its limit in respect for others."

The Vatican's intervention adds a new dimension to a debate that has already drawn sharp criticism from conservative Christian groups and political figures. House SpeakerMike Johnson, a LouisianaRepublican, described the performance as a "mockery" that was "insulting to Christian people around the world." Senator Katie Britt, an Alabama Republican, called it "disgraceful," while RepresentativeLauren Boebertof Colorado labeled it "disgusting."

Former presidentDonald Trumpadded to the controversy duringan interview with Laura Ingrahamon FOX News this week by condemning the event, "I thought that the opening ceremony was a disgrace, actually," said Trump. "I thought it was a disgrace."

However, the controversy has alsosparked a broader discussionabout artistic interpretation and cultural representation. Thomas Jolly, the show's artistic director, told theAssociated Pressthat the intention was not to be subversive or offensive, but rather to "send a message of love, a message of inclusion and not at all to divide."

Loadingtwittercontent...

Some religious leaders have offered alternative interpretations of the scene. Atlanta PastorCassie Noland Rapko suggested on Facebookthat the tableau represented the Feast of Dionysus, not the Last Supper, noting the Olympics' Greek origins and France's cultural emphasis on festivity and the performing arts.

In response to the growing controversy, the Paris 2024 organizing committee issued an apology on July 28, stating there was never an intention to disrespect any religious group. The International Olympic Committee (IOC) also released a statement welcoming this clarification and reiterating that any offense caused was unintentional.

title: Vatican Issues ‘Unusual’ Response to Olympics ‘Last Supper’ Controversy - Newsweek
description: Vatican expresses disappointment over Paris Olympics opening ceremony scene, joining critics who saw it as mocking Christianity.
